findings.summary: Codex-generated config drops the enforcing PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks, so the command guard, secret scanner, linter and comment checker never run — only advisory, model-optional yaah MCP tools remain.
Command guard uses a narrow regex denylist that misses long-form and reordered flag variants (rm --recursive --force /, rm -fr /, git push -f), so catastrophic commands bypass the block.

The context7 MCP server is fetched unpinned via `npx -y @context7/mcp` and auto-installed fresh on every run, violating the remit's supply-chain pinning MUST (rug-pull / dependency-confusion surface).
The secret scanner runs on PostToolUse, so a credential is already written to disk before it is detected and blocked, contravening the requirement to detect secrets before an edit is accepted.

The structured session audit never records security findings — the Session.Findings channel is declared and printed by `session show` but never populated, so scan results survive only as free text on blocked calls.

Generated session-loaded files (skills, agents, AGENTS.md) are written world-readable/writable with no integrity or review mechanism — a memory/persistence-poisoning surface, though yaah itself does no inference so no injection path is confirmed.

The yaah_planning_init MCP tool writes workspace scaffolding with no in-server approval gate, relying entirely on host confirmation, despite the remit requiring workspace writes to reach a human checkpoint.
The notion MCP provider — absent from the remit's authorized counterparties — embeds a live bearer token directly into generated MCP config env when enabled and is fetched unpinned via `npx -y`.

The protective handlers (command guard, secret scanner) have no unit or adversarial tests, and nothing validates the 'every generated config carries the full protection set' promise, so the Codex protection gap ships undetected.
